channel.title: Jiwoo Shin
channel.description: AI research notes and paper reviews by Jiwoo Shin

item.title: [Model Review] DiffusionGemma: How DeepMind Made Uniform-State Diffusion Work at Scale
item.dc:creator: Jiwoo Shin
item.description:
Model: Google DeepMind, DiffusionGemma (26B MoE, ~3.8B active), released June 2026. Official page · Developer guide · Docs & explainer · Weights on Hugging Face

TL;DR

For the past two years the discrete-diffusion language-model literature has had a settled hierarchy: masked (absorbing-state) diffusion works, uniform-state diffusion doesn’t. Masked diffusion gives the model an explicit signal about which tokens need fixing, and essentially every competitive diffusion LM — MDLM, LLaDA, and friends — has been built on it. So it was genuinely surprising that DiffusionGemma, DeepMind’s first open diffusion language model, is built on uniform-state diffusion, where corruption replaces tokens with random vocabulary tokens rather than a [MASK] symbol.

The reason for the bet is the one property masked diffusion structurally lacks: self-correction. In masked diffusion, once a mask is resolved into a word, it is locked in forever. In uniform-state diffusion, any position can be re-noised and revised when later context invalidates it. DiffusionGemma makes that property practical with four moves: (1) it doesn’t train from scratch but adapts a strong autoregressive checkpoint (Gemma 4 26B A4B), sidestepping the hardest part of the uniform-state training problem; (2) a single backbone that toggles between causal and bidirectional attention, so prompts are prefetched into a KV cache autoregressively while 256-token “canvases” are denoised in parallel; (3) self-conditioning, feeding the previous step’s full token distribution forward so aggressive re-noising doesn’t cause oscillation; and (4) an entropy-bounded sampler that accepts only confident tokens each step, re-noises the rest, and stops adaptively — typically in 12–16 steps. The payoff is 1,000+ tokens/second on a single H100 (~4× autoregressive) with a visible ability to fix its own mistakes mid-generation; the cost is a 5–15 point drop on reasoning-heavy benchmarks versus its own autoregressive parent.

Background: why masked diffusion was supposed to win

Discrete diffusion LMs replace left-to-right decoding with an iterative denoising loop: corrupt text with noise in a forward process, train a model to invert it, then generate by starting from pure noise and denoising a whole block of tokens in parallel. The design choice that defines everything downstream is what the noise is.

Masked (absorbing-state) diffusion corrupts by replacing tokens with a special [MASK] token. The forward process for a token interpolates toward a point mass on the mask:

Uniform-state diffusion corrupts by resampling tokens uniformly from the vocabulary:

The masked version has dominated for a mundane but decisive reason: the model always knows which tokens are noise, because they are literally labeled [MASK]. Denoising reduces to a BERT-style infilling problem with a clean, low-variance training objective. In the uniform-state version, a corrupted token looks like any other token. As the official explainer’s companion analysis puts it, the training objective “does not only need to denoise canvases at various noise levels, it also needs to first identify which tokens are actually noise in the first place.” That extra burden shows up as high-variance gradients and consistently worse perplexity — hence the conventional wisdom.

But masked diffusion buys its trainability with a structural flaw: absorption is one-way. Once the sampler commits a mask to a real token, there is no mechanism to un-commit it. If an early, high-confidence guess turns out to clash with context that emerges three steps later, the model must write around the mistake rather than fix it. For a paradigm whose whole selling point is “generate 256 tokens in parallel and iteratively refine,” refinement that can never touch already-decided tokens is a strange kind of refinement.

Uniform-state diffusion doesn’t have that flaw. Since noise and signal live in the same space — both are just vocabulary tokens — the reverse process is free to reconsider every position at every step. A token that looked right at step 3 can be demoted back to noise at step 7. That is the property DeepMind decided was worth the training pain.

What DiffusionGemma actually is

DiffusionGemma is a 26B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model activating ~3.8B parameters per forward pass, built on the Gemma 4 architecture with up to a 256K context window. Generation is block-autoregressive: the model denoises a 256-token canvas in parallel, commits the finished block to the KV cache, opens a fresh canvas, and repeats. So it is diffusion within blocks and autoregression across them — a hybrid that keeps parallel generation while still scaling to long outputs. The lineage, per Google, runs through the (never-released) Gemini Diffusion research line.

The four ingredients that make the uniform-state part work are worth walking through one by one.

1. Don’t train uniform-state diffusion from scratch — adapt an AR model

The hardest part of uniform-state training is the noise-identification problem above, and DiffusionGemma largely sidesteps it: rather than pretraining a diffusion model from random init, it starts from the pretrained autoregressive Gemma 4 26B A4B checkpoint and adapts it to the denoising objective. A model that already has strong language priors doesn’t need to learn what real text looks like before it can tell noise from signal — implausible tokens in context are already low-probability under the prior. The from-scratch identification burden becomes a much easier fine-tuning problem: recognize where the canvas is inconsistent with fluent text, and fix it.

This mirrors what the broader field converged on for masked diffusion (adaptation recipes turning AR checkpoints into diffusion models), but it matters more for uniform-state, because the objective it rescues is the noisier one.

2. One backbone, two attention modes

A naive diffusion LM throws away the KV cache — bidirectional attention over a changing canvas has nothing stable to cache, which is disastrous for long prompts. DiffusionGemma’s answer is an encoder–denoiser patch on a single decoder-only backbone that dynamically toggles between two modes:

  • Prefill / incremental prefill (causal): the prompt — and each completed 256-token block — is processed with ordinary causal attention and written into a KV cache, exactly like an AR model.
  • Denoising (bidirectional): every canvas position attends to all other canvas positions and reads the cached prefix. Information flows symmetrically across the block being generated.

This is the piece that makes the whole thing deployable: prompts are cached once, finished text is cached incrementally, and only the live 256-token canvas pays the bidirectional price. It’s also why the speed numbers hold up — each denoising step is one forward pass that advances all 256 positions at once, shifting single-user inference from memory-bound to compute-bound.

3. Self-conditioning: carry the distribution, not the sample

Aggressive re-noising creates an obvious failure mode: oscillation. If the sampler keeps resetting low-confidence positions to fresh random tokens, the model can end up relitigating the same positions forever, since each step only sees the current (partly re-randomized) canvas and remembers nothing about its previous beliefs.

DiffusionGemma stabilizes this with self-conditioning: instead of discarding the previous step’s output, it takes the full softmax distribution over the vocabulary at each position, converts it into a probability-weighted average of token embeddings, and feeds that into the next denoising step alongside the canvas. Soft information — “I was 60/40 between these two candidates” — survives the hard re-noising of the sampled token. The model refines a persistent belief state rather than repeatedly re-deciding from scratch, which is what lets convergence happen in ~12–16 steps instead of drifting.

4. The entropy-bounded sampler: accept the confident, re-noise the rest

The default sampler makes the self-correction concrete. Each step:

  1. The denoiser produces a distribution at every canvas position; the sampler computes each position’s entropy.
  2. Positions are sorted from most to least confident, and tokens are accepted greedily until an accumulated entropy budget (default 0.1) is exhausted — so the number of tokens committed per step adapts to how sure the model is, rather than being a fixed schedule.
  3. Everything not accepted is re-noised back to a random token (via Gumbel-max sampling) and gets reconsidered next step — including positions that were accepted in earlier steps but whose probability has since collapsed under new context.
  4. A temperature schedule decays from 0.8 to 0.4 over the trajectory (explore early, commit late), and adaptive stopping halts generation when canvas entropy drops below 0.005 and predictions are stable across consecutive steps — typically 12–16 steps against a 48-step maximum.

Step 3 is the payoff of the entire uniform-state bet. In the developer guide’s Sudoku demo, you can watch digits that were committed early get demoted back to noise when a later-resolved row makes them illegal — the model literally erases and rewrites its own output. No masked-diffusion sampler can do that.

Does the bet pay off?

On speed, unambiguously. 1,000+ tokens/second on a single H100, 700+ on an RTX 5090, quoted as up to 4× faster than comparable AR generation; quantized, the MoE fits in roughly 18–24 GB of VRAM, i.e. one consumer GPU. There’s native NVFP4 support for Blackwell and day-one vLLM integration.

On self-correction, demonstrably. The Sudoku case study is the cleanest evidence: the base model solves ~0% of puzzles, but a short supervised fine-tune with the released JAX recipe lifts it to ~80% — while cutting denoising steps from 48 to 12, because a specialized model stabilizes faster and adaptive stopping notices. Constraint-satisfaction tasks, where a decision at position 40 depends on position 200, are exactly where bidirectional attention plus revisable commitments should shine, and they do.

On raw capability, honestly, no — not yet. The model card’s own numbers against its autoregressive parent:

Benchmark DiffusionGemma 26B Gemma 4 26B
MMLU Pro 77.6 82.6
GPQA Diamond 73.2 82.3
BigBench Extra Hard 47.6 64.8
MATH-Vision 70.5 82.4

A 5-point gap on knowledge-flavored suites, widening to 17 points on BigBench Extra Hard. The pattern is coherent: the further a task is from “refine a locally-checkable block of text” and the closer to “long serial dependent reasoning,” the more the parallel-canvas paradigm gives up. DeepMind positions the model accordingly — low-latency, low-concurrency, local and interactive use — rather than claiming AR parity.

Two independent caveats are worth knowing before over-romanticizing the mechanism. An inference-time interpretability study of the released checkpoint (arXiv:2606.14620) found that token commit order retains a moderate left-to-right bias (Kendall τ ≈ 0.43–0.60) — the model is “neither strictly parallel nor sequential,” so a chunk of the AR inductive bias survives adaptation. And the confidence signal driving the entropy-bounded sampler is well-calibrated on math (AUROC ≈ 0.75) but near chance on factual recall (≈ 0.47): “low entropy” reliably means “correct” only in some domains, which is a real limitation for a sampler whose acceptance rule is an entropy threshold.

The research context: uniform-state was already being rehabilitated

DeepMind’s documentation doesn’t cite academic lineage, but DiffusionGemma didn’t come out of nowhere — the “uniform-state is hopeless” consensus had been eroding in the open literature for a year. The key work is The Diffusion Duality (Sahoo et al., ICML 2025, arXiv:2506.10892), which showed that uniform-state discrete diffusion emerges from an underlying Gaussian diffusion (discrete tokens as the argmax of Gaussian latents), and used that duality to transfer the continuous-diffusion toolbox — a variance-reducing curriculum that doubled training speed, and consistency distillation for few-step generation — to the discrete setting, beating AR models on zero-shot perplexity on 3 of 7 benchmarks. Its ICLR 2026 sequel (Ψ-samplers, arXiv:2602.21185) added predictor–corrector samplers under which uniform-state models keep improving with more steps, explicitly challenging the assumption that “masked diffusion is the inevitable future of diffusion-based language modeling.”

I want to be careful here: there is no public evidence DiffusionGemma uses Duo’s techniques, and its robustness recipe (AR adaptation + self-conditioning + entropy-bounded sampling) is a different toolkit than Duo’s (Gaussian curriculum + distillation). But both attack the same two weaknesses of uniform-state diffusion — noisy training signal, wasteful sampling — and DiffusionGemma is best read as the first at-scale, productionized confirmation of the same thesis: uniform-state’s self-correction is real, and its training problems are fixable.

My take

The main contribution here is not a new equation — every component has precedent — but a existence proof at 26B scale: the first open-weights demonstration that uniform-state diffusion, the formulation the field wrote off, becomes competitive once you stop trying to win the pretraining objective on its own terms. The load-bearing insight is that uniform-state’s noise-identification problem is only crippling from scratch; initialized from a strong AR prior, “which tokens are noise” is nearly free, and what you keep is the one capability masked diffusion can’t have. That reframing — treat the formulation choice as a sampler capability question rather than a perplexity question — is the intellectually interesting move.

I also think the block-autoregressive design deserves more credit than it gets in the headlines. Pure-diffusion LMs kept dying on deployment realities (no KV cache, fixed-length generation); diffusion-inside-blocks, AR-across-blocks quietly concedes that autoregression is right at the document scale while diffusion is right at the paragraph scale. That feels like the shape future hybrids will keep.

The skeptical read is equally available: the model trails its own AR parent everywhere except speed, its “parallelism” is partially left-to-right in disguise, and its confidence signal — the heart of the sampler — is unreliable off-distribution. If your workload is cloud-batched, high-QPS serving, batching already gives AR models their parallelism back, and DiffusionGemma’s advantage largely evaporates. The honest pitch is narrower and still compelling: single-user, latency-bound, local inference — the setting where an AR model leaves the GPU 95% idle — plus structured tasks where mid-generation revision genuinely matters. Whether the capability gap closes with diffusion-native pretraining at larger scale, rather than adaptation, is now the obvious next question, and for the first time there’s an open model to build that answer on.

Related

  • The model: DiffusionGemma official page · developer guide · how-it-works explainer · model card
  • Best independent walkthrough: Maarten Grootendorst, A Visual Guide to DiffusionGemma
  • The uniform-state rehabilitation line: Sahoo et al., The Diffusion Duality, ICML 2025 — arXiv:2506.10892; Chapter II: Ψ-Samplers, ICLR 2026 — arXiv:2602.21185
  • The critical lens: inference-time analysis of DiffusionGemma’s decoding behavior — arXiv:2606.14620
  • Training recipes: Hackable Diffusion (JAX) · vLLM integration

item.title: [Paper Review] Block Diffusion: Interpolating Between Autoregressive and Diffusion Language Models
item.dc:creator: Jiwoo Shin
item.description:
Paper: Marianne Arriola, Aaron Gokaslan, Justin T. Chiu, Zhihan Yang, Zhixuan Qi, Jiaqi Han, Subham Sekhar Sahoo, Volodymyr Kuleshov. Block Diffusion: Interpolating Between Autoregressive and Diffusion Language Models. ICLR 2025. arXiv:2503.09573 · project page · code

TL;DR

Diffusion language models circa 2024 had three chronic problems: they could only generate sequences of a fixed, training-time length; they couldn’t use a KV cache, so they re-encoded the entire sequence at every denoising step; and their perplexity lagged autoregressive models. BD3-LMs (Block Discrete Denoising Diffusion Language Models) attack all three with one structural move: be autoregressive over blocks, diffusion within blocks. Generate a block of tokens by parallel denoising, freeze it, cache its keys and values exactly like an AR model caches tokens, then move to the next block — which restores both the KV cache and arbitrary-length generation in one stroke.

The subtler half of the paper is about the perplexity gap, and it contains the result I find most instructive: a BD3-LM with block size 1 is mathematically equivalent to an AR model in expectation, yet trains to a two-point-worse perplexity. The culprit is not the objective but the variance of its Monte Carlo estimator — and the noise schedule, which famously doesn’t affect the diffusion ELBO’s value, turns out to control that variance entirely. Their fix, clipped schedules that only sample masking rates inside a data-driven band , cuts gradient variance dramatically and sets a then-state-of-the-art perplexity for discrete diffusion (within ~15% of AR on OpenWebText). If you read my DiffusionGemma post, this paper is the academic blueprint of that model’s block-autoregressive skeleton — worth understanding on its own terms.

The setup: two paradigms, complementary failure modes

An autoregressive model factorizes and pays for its quality with strict seriality: tokens take forward passes. A discrete diffusion model (D3PM-style, and its masked-diffusion refinements like MDLM) denoises all positions in parallel — but it commits to a canvas of fixed width at training time, and because every position is still in flux at every step, nothing can ever be cached.

BD3-LM’s move is to put a dial between the two. Partition the sequence into blocks of length and factorize

where each conditional is itself a masked diffusion model over just the tokens of block , conditioned on all clean, finished previous blocks. Set and you recover an AR model; set and you recover vanilla diffusion; everything in between is new territory. One transformer with a block-causal attention mask parameterizes all conditionals at once: tokens in block attend to blocks through , bidirectionally within their own block, causally across blocks.

Here is the metaphor I’ll use for the rest of the post. An AR model is a typewriter: one keystroke at a time, ink permanent, and the growing manuscript sits in a binder next to you for reference. A vanilla diffusion model is a whiteboard: the whole essay is sketched at once in erasable marker and revised in sweeps — wonderfully parallel, but since any word might still change, you can never file anything away, and every revision pass means re-reading the entire board. BD3-LM writes on whiteboard panels the size of a paragraph: draft the current panel in erasable marker, and the moment it’s done, laminate it, file it in the binder, and start a fresh panel. The binder is the KV cache.

Part I: why diffusion models couldn’t have a KV cache — and how blocks restore it

Caching requires immutability

The KV cache is such a fixture of AR inference that it’s easy to forget what makes it valid: keys and values for position can be stored and reused only because token never changes again. The cache is a ledger of settled facts. Vanilla diffusion violates this at every step — position 3 at denoising step 7 may hold a different token than at step 6, and with bidirectional attention its key/value vectors also depend on every other position, which is also changing. Every entry in the would-be cache is stale the moment you write it. So diffusion models recompute attention over all positions at all steps: the whole whiteboard, re-read for every sweep of the marker.

Block diffusion restores the ledger by restoring immutability — at block granularity. During generation (their Algorithm 2):

  1. Denoise block ’s canvas over some number of diffusion steps, with cross-attention into the cached keys/values of all finished blocks.
  2. When the block is done, run one clean forward pass over it to produce , and append them to the cache — laminate the panel, file it.
  3. Open the next canvas.

The finished prefix is now exactly as cheap as it is for an AR model, while the live canvas keeps diffusion’s parallelism (all tokens per denoising step). And since blocks are appended one after another with no fixed horizon, generation runs until an [EOS] appears — the fixed-length straitjacket is gone for free. In their OpenWebText experiments this is dramatic: SEDD, trained on 1024-token contexts, can emit at most 1024 tokens; BD3-LM sampled documents up to ~9,982 tokens — roughly 10× its training context — because “context length” now only bounds the binder the model was trained to read, not the number of panels it can file.

The training-time twist: every block must be seen twice

There’s a wrinkle that makes training less obvious than sampling, and the paper’s engineering contribution lives here. To compute the loss for block you need the model’s denoising prediction on the noisy — but to condition blocks you need keys/values from the clean . Same block, two roles, two different inputs. A single standard forward pass can’t produce both, so naively you’d loop over blocks times.

Their vectorized solution: concatenate the noisy and clean sequences into one input of length and run a single forward pass under a specialized attention mask stitched from three pieces:

  • (block-diagonal): noisy tokens attend to noisy tokens within their own block — the live panel is coherent with itself;
  • (offset block-causal): noisy tokens attend to clean tokens of strictly earlier blocks — the panel may consult the binder, but only the laminated pages, never a clean copy of itself (that would be the answer key for the very exercise being graded);
  • (block-causal): clean tokens attend to clean tokens of their own and earlier blocks — this builds the binder itself.

The resulting mask is extremely sparse, and they exploit that with a fused FlexAttention kernel: up to ~5× faster than a naively masked scaled_dot_product_attention on the attention op, ~20–25% end-to-end training speedup over the two-pass alternative, and overall training that stays within 2× the cost of ordinary diffusion training. Each token still passes through the network twice in effect — that’s the irreducible price of playing both student and answer key — but the two passes share one kernel launch and one attention computation.

Part II: the noise schedule is a variance knob

This is the half of the paper I’d assign to students. It starts with an experiment that should be a null result and isn’t.

The puzzle: an equivalence that fails in practice

Set . Every “block” is a single token, the within-block diffusion collapses, and the paper proves (their Suppl. B.4) that the block-diffusion NELBO is equal in expectation to the AR negative log-likelihood. Same model class, same objective in expectation — training should give the same perplexity. It doesn’t: on LM1B, AR reaches 22.88 while the BD3-LM stalls at ≤ 25.56.

The diagnosis is beautiful in its mundanity. Under the standard linear masking schedule, the mask rate at a uniformly sampled time averages 50% — so on any given batch, the diffusion loss is computed only on the ~half of tokens that happen to be masked. The AR model gets a gradient signal from every token in the batch; the diffusion model gets a gradient estimated from a random half. Both estimators are unbiased. One is twice as noisy. The authors make the point with a killer control: train an AR model with a randomized batch size and its perplexity degrades to 24.37 — most of the way to the diffusion number. The “diffusion gap” at was never about diffusion; it was about gradient variance.

The metaphor: imagine two teachers estimating a student’s ability. One grades the entire exam every week. The other rolls a die and grades a random subset of questions — sometimes three questions, sometimes forty. Their long-run averages agree exactly, but the second teacher’s weekly reports swing wildly, and if you adjust your teaching (your gradient step) after every report, the noisy reports send you zigzagging. SGD is that teacher: what matters isn’t only the expected loss but how much the estimate jitters batch to batch.

And here’s the twist that turns diagnosis into cure: for , if you change the forward process to always mask the token (), the diffusion objective becomes literally next-token prediction — every “worksheet” is exactly the AR exercise — and the perplexity gap closes to zero: 22.88, matching AR to the second decimal. Measured NELBO variance drops from 1.52 to 0.11. The schedule didn’t change what the model was learning in expectation; it changed how noisily the model was told about it.

Why extreme mask rates are wasted worksheets

For , always-mask-everything is no longer the answer (the model must learn to denoise partial corruption to sample well). But the variance logic generalizes into an intuition about which masking rates are worth training on. Think of each training example as a fill-in-the-blank worksheet, with the schedule deciding how many blanks:

  • Almost no blanks: the exercise is copy-the-context; reconstruction is trivial, loss ≈ 0, gradient ≈ nothing. A wasted worksheet.
  • All blanks: with no context at all inside the block, the best possible answer is the marginal statistics of the language — guess “the,” guess “of.” That’s learnable in the first hour of training and never again informative. Also wasted.
  • The middle band: enough context to reason from, enough blanks to be forced to. All the learning signal lives here.

A linear schedule samples mask rates uniformly on , so it keeps printing worksheets from both wasted extremes — and those near-zero-signal batches are precisely what inflates the variance of the gradient estimator. The fix is almost embarrassingly simple: clipped schedules, which sample the mask rate and simply refuse to train outside the productive band.

The subtle part — and the reason this is a contribution rather than a hack — is the theoretical status of the schedule. The masked-diffusion NELBO is provably invariant to the noise schedule in expectation (a known MDLM-era result), which had licensed everyone to treat the schedule as a don’t-care. The paper’s point is that this invariance holds for the value of the objective, not for the variance of its Monte Carlo estimator. The schedule is not a modeling choice at all; it is a variance-reduction dial hiding in plain sight. (Two teachers again: both unbiased, but you get to choose which one grades you.)

Data-driven clipping: the right band depends on the block size

Where should sit? Not anywhere universal. Their Table 8 is worth staring at: for , heavy masking is best; for , a moderate band wins. The pattern makes sense through the worksheet lens: a small block sits inside lots of clean, laminated left-context, so even a nearly-blank worksheet is still solvable from the binder — you can crank the difficulty up. A big block is more self-referential, so all-blanks degenerates toward marginal-guessing faster — the productive band sits lower.

Rather than hand-tune this, they make it adaptive: since gradient variance itself is expensive to estimate, they use the variance of the NELBO estimator as a proxy, and grid-search to minimize it at every validation epoch (~every 5K steps) during training. Variance of the loss turns out to correlate cleanly with final test perplexity across schedules — which is itself a nice empirical finding: you can select schedules by a statistic that never touches held-out data.

Does it work?

On likelihoods, yes, and monotonically in the direction the theory predicts (smaller blocks → closer to AR): on LM1B (65B tokens), BD3-LM reaches PPL ≤ 30.60 / 29.83 / 28.23 for , versus MDLM’s 31.78 — up to a 13% improvement and a new discrete-diffusion state of the art at the time. On OpenWebText (524B tokens): ≤ 22.27 / 21.68 / 20.73 versus MDLM’s 22.98, with AR at 17.54. The gap to AR is narrowed, not closed — the NELBO is provably looser as grows, so some of the remaining gap is structural.

On sample quality, the ordering is the same: generative perplexity 25.7 () versus MDLM’s 46.8 at matched compute (1K function evaluations), and an order of magnitude fewer network calls than SSD-LM, the earlier Gaussian-embedding block-diffusion approach, at far better quality. And the arbitrary-length capability is qualitative: ~10× longer samples than any fixed-canvas diffusion baseline.

My take

The lasting contribution here isn’t the architecture — “semi-autoregressive” generation existed — it’s two conceptual clarifications that outlived the specific model. First, the observation that caching and revision are fundamentally in tension, and that the block is the natural truce line: revise freely inside the panel, laminate at the boundary. Every deployed diffusion LM I’m aware of has since adopted exactly this shape — DiffusionGemma’s 256-token canvases committed to a KV cache, with causal/bidirectional mode switching, is this paper’s Figure-1 diagram productionized (with the within-block noise swapped from masking to uniform-state, trading the tight NELBO for self-correction — the two posts are really about the two halves of one design space). It’s also no coincidence: BD3-LM comes from the Kuleshov group, whose Diffusion Duality line supplied the uniform-state half of that story too.

Second, and more transferable: “invariant in expectation” is not “irrelevant in practice.” The noise-schedule result is a small masterclass in a failure pattern that recurs all over ML — an objective is proven invariant to some choice, the community stops thinking about that choice, and the choice quietly dominates optimization through second-order statistics. The experiment is the cleanest demonstration I’ve seen: two provably-equivalent objectives, a two-point perplexity gap, and a one-line schedule change that closes it exactly. If you keep one thing from this paper, keep the habit of asking not “does this choice change my loss?” but “does it change the variance of my estimator of my loss?”

The honest limitations are the ones the authors state: training costs up to ~2× standard diffusion (every block plays both student and answer key), small blocks reinherit AR’s seriality, and the optimal block size is task-dependent with no principle yet for choosing it. And the perplexity gap to AR, while narrowed, remains — closed only in the degenerate limit where you’ve rebuilt an AR model. Interpolation, it turns out, means inheriting a weighted average of the weaknesses too. But as a piece of analysis that turned “diffusion LMs are worse and nobody quite knows why” into “here is the variance, here is the knob, here is the recipe,” it’s one of the most useful diffusion-LM papers of its year.

Related

  • The paper: Arriola et al. Block Diffusion: Interpolating Between Autoregressive and Diffusion Language Models. ICLR 2025 — arXiv:2503.09573 · project page & blog · code
  • The masked-diffusion foundation it builds on: Sahoo et al. Simple and Effective Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLM), NeurIPS 2024 — OpenReview
  • Where the design shipped: my review of DiffusionGemma, whose block-autoregressive canvas + KV-cache design is this paper’s skeleton at 26B scale
  • The same group’s uniform-state line: Sahoo et al. The Diffusion Duality, ICML 2025 — arXiv:2506.10892
  • The Gaussian-embedding predecessor it improves on: Han et al. SSD-LM, 2022 — arXiv:2210.17432

item.title: [Paper Review] Coconut: Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space
item.dc:creator: Jiwoo Shin
item.description:
Paper: Shibo Hao, Sainbayar Sukhbaatar, DiJia Su, Xian Li, Zhiting Hu, Jason Weston, Yuandong Tian. Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space. COLM 2025. arXiv:2412.06769

TL;DR

Chain-of-thought forces a model to think in words, but most tokens in a reasoning trace are there for grammatical glue, not computation — and words force the model to commit to one path at each step. Coconut (Chain of Continuous Thought) removes the words: it takes the LLM’s last hidden state and feeds it back in as the next input embedding, so the model reasons in its own continuous representation space instead of decoding to text and re-encoding. The payoff is that a continuous thought is not a single token but a distribution — it can hold several candidate next steps at once, letting the model do something like a breadth-first search over a reasoning graph rather than greedily picking one branch. On planning-heavy logical tasks Coconut matches or beats CoT while emitting far fewer tokens; the cost is a fiddly multi-stage training curriculum and reasoning traces no human can read.

Why this matters

Chain-of-thought works, but it is a strange thing to demand of a model. To “reason,” the model must serialize its internal state into a sequence of natural-language tokens, then re-read those tokens to continue. Two problems fall out of this. First, it is wasteful: in a sentence like “so the next number we need to check is 12, which means…”, the tokens carrying reasoning content are a small fraction of the whole; the rest is textual coherence. Second, and more fundamentally, language is committal. Sampling a token is a hard decision — the moment the model writes “12,” it has thrown away the alternative that it should have checked 15. For problems that require search or backtracking, this premature commitment is exactly the failure mode.

Coconut’s premise is that the reasoning state does not need to pass through the vocabulary bottleneck at all. The natural place for a transformer to keep its “state” is the hidden vector it already computes — a point in , not a one-hot over the vocabulary. Why round that vector to the nearest word and then re-embed it, when you can just hand it back to the model directly?

The core idea: feed the hidden state back, don’t decode it

In ordinary autoregressive decoding, the input embedding at each step comes from the previously generated token. Coconut adds a second mode. Special markers <bot> and <eot> (begin/end of thought) bracket a stretch of latent reasoning. Inside that stretch, the input embedding at step is not a token embedding — it is the last hidden state from the previous step:

where is the final-layer hidden state produced at the previous position. No softmax, no token sampling, no re-embedding — the “continuous thought” is just passed forward. After a fixed number of such latent steps, the model emits <eot> and returns to normal language decoding to produce the answer.

The conceptual consequence is the whole point. A decoded token is a single choice; a hidden state is a dense vector that can carry a superposition of possibilities. The authors show, on a graph-reachability planning task, that the continuous thought behaves like the frontier of a breadth-first search: early latent steps keep weight on several candidate next nodes simultaneously, and later steps prune the dead ends. CoT, by contrast, must gamble on one node and hope it can recover if wrong. Reasoning in latent space lets the model defer commitment — it explores in parallel and only collapses to a definite answer when it decodes back to language.

Training: a curriculum that dissolves the chain of thought

You cannot supervise a continuous thought directly — there is no ground-truth hidden vector to regress toward. Coconut is trained instead by a multi-stage curriculum that gradually replaces language reasoning with latent reasoning, starting from ordinary CoT data.

At stage , the model trains on full written CoT (question, all reasoning steps, answer) as usual. At stage , the first reasoning steps are removed from the text and replaced by continuous thoughts, where is the number of latent thoughts substituted per step (a small constant). The loss is computed only on the remaining text — the later reasoning steps and the final answer. The continuous thoughts receive no direct loss; gradients reach them by backpropagating through the recurrence from the tokens that follow. Over stages, more and more of the written reasoning is swallowed into latent space, and the model learns to carry that computation in instead of in words.

One consequence worth flagging for anyone tempted to reproduce it: because each continuous thought depends on the hidden state of the one before, the latent segment cannot be parallelized across positions the way teacher-forced token training can. Generating continuous thoughts requires sequential forward passes, which makes training meaningfully more expensive than standard CoT fine-tuning. This is the practical tax on the whole idea.

Results

The evaluation is deliberately built around tasks where search matters: GSM8K for math word problems, and two synthetic logical-reasoning benchmarks, ProntoQA and the harder ProsQA (a graph-structured reachability task designed to demand real planning rather than pattern completion).

The headline pattern is a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off. On the planning-heavy tasks, Coconut with enough continuous thoughts per step matches or exceeds written CoT while generating dramatically fewer tokens at inference — the reasoning happens in latent space, so the model does not pay to spell it out. On ProsQA in particular, where the branching structure rewards keeping options open, latent reasoning pulls clearly ahead of CoT; adding more continuous thoughts per step continues to help, consistent with the BFS interpretation. On GSM8K the story is more nuanced — CoT’s explicit arithmetic is a strong baseline — but Coconut still beats a no-reasoning baseline and lands close to CoT at a fraction of the token count.

The interpretability probe is, to me, the most convincing evidence. When the authors decode what the continuous thoughts would have said, they find the latent state maintaining a spread of plausible next nodes and narrowing it over successive steps — a mechanistic picture of the parallel-then-prune behavior, rather than a claim inferred only from accuracy numbers.

My take

What I like about Coconut is that it isolates a single, clean idea and follows it honestly: the vocabulary is a bottleneck on reasoning, so route around it. The superposition-enabling-search framing is genuinely illuminating — it reframes CoT’s weakness not as “too few tokens” but as “premature discretization,” which is a more useful diagnosis. It also sits in an interesting relationship with the broader push toward inference-time compute: everyone is trying to buy more “thinking,” and Coconut argues some of that thinking is cheaper and better done in latent space than in text.

The honest caveats are real, though. The sequential training cost is the obvious one, and it partly explains why the paper stays at the scale it does. The curriculum is delicate — the method is a recipe as much as an architecture, and how gracefully it transfers to large pretrained reasoners without heavy per-task staging is still open. And the interpretability loss cuts against a major reason CoT became popular in the first place: a readable trace you can audit. A model that reasons in vectors is, by construction, harder to oversee.

The reason I’m reviewing an early-2025 paper as a “hot” pick is that the line it opened is unmistakably where a chunk of 2026 reasoning research now lives. The continuous-latent-reasoning direction — looped and recurrent-depth transformers, “pondering” in embedding space, and theoretical work on why continuous chains of thought are more expressive than discrete ones (the superposition analyses) — all trace back to this move. Coconut isn’t the last word on latent reasoning; it’s the paper that made the question respectable. Read it for the idea, not the leaderboard.

Related

  • The paper: Hao, Sukhbaatar, Su, Li, Hu, Weston & Tian. Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space. COLM 2025 — arXiv:2412.06769
  • The contrast it’s arguing with: Wei et al. Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models. NeurIPS 2022 — arXiv:2201.11903
  • Where the line went next: a growing 2025–2026 literature on latent/looped reasoning and the training dynamics of continuous chain-of-thought (e.g. the “emergence of superposition” analyses).

item.title: [Paper Review] Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis (Diffuser)
item.dc:creator: Jiwoo Shin
item.description:
Paper: Michael Janner*, Yilun Du*, Joshua B. Tenenbaum, Sergey Levine. Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis. ICML 2022. arXiv:2205.09991 · PMLR · Project page · Code

TL;DR

Model-based RL usually means: learn a one-step dynamics model, then hand it to a classical trajectory optimizer. In practice the optimizer exploits the model’s errors, producing adversarial plans rather than optimal ones. Diffuser dissolves the model/planner boundary: train a denoising diffusion model directly over entire trajectories — a 2D array of states and actions — so that planning becomes almost identical to sampling. A plan is not rolled out step by step; it emerges whole, refined from noise over the denoising iterations.

Two conditioning mechanisms make the samples useful for control, both borrowed from image diffusion: rewards enter as classifier-guided sampling (gradients of a learned return model nudge the denoising mean), and constraints — most importantly the initial and final states of the trajectory — enter as inpainting: the constrained entries of the array are simply overwritten with their fixed values after every denoising step, exactly like known pixels in image inpainting. That second mechanism is the answer to a question I had when reading: can you fix where a generated trajectory starts and ends? Here it is not a trick bolted on afterwards — it is how every plan is conditioned on the current state, and how goal-reaching is solved with zero reward signal. On the sparse-reward Maze2D benchmark, this start/goal inpainting alone scores 119.5 average (vs 47.0 for the best model-free baseline), and the same trained model transfers to randomized goals without any retraining — you just clamp a different terminal state.

Why this problem matters

Trajectory optimization is the cleanest story in control: write down the objective, let the optimizer find the actions. But it needs ground-truth dynamics . The learning-based workaround — approximate with a neural network, plug it into the optimizer — fails in a characteristic way: powerful optimizers treat model error as opportunity, steering into regions where the learned model hallucinates high reward. So practical model-based RL retreated to weak gradient-free planners, or abandoned planning for value functions.

Diffuser’s proposal: if the planner is going to be tightly coupled to the model anyway, design the model around the planning problem. Predict full trajectories non-autoregressively (no compounding one-step error), model actions jointly with states (a plan needs both), stay reward-agnostic (one model, many tasks), and make the sampling procedure itself the planner.

The diffusion model: planning as sampling

Trajectories as arrays. Diffuser represents a plan as a two-dimensional array — one column per planning timestep, states stacked on actions:

The model learns with a standard diffusion setup (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020): a forward process corrupts trajectories with Gaussian noise over diffusion steps , and a learned reverse process denoises them back. Training is the usual simplified -prediction objective with a cosine noise schedule. Notation to keep straight: superscripts are diffusion time (noise level), subscripts are planning time.

Why generation must be non-autoregressive. This is my favorite conceptual point in the paper. Dynamics prediction is causal — the present follows from the past — but decision-making is anti-causal: what I should do now depends on where I want to end up. Formally, goal-conditioned inference conditions on the future. A left-to-right autoregressive model structurally cannot do this; a diffusion model that generates all timesteps concurrently can. This is precisely the property that makes terminal-state conditioning possible at all — information from the clamped end of the trajectory propagates backwards through planning time during denoising.

Architecture: local steps, global coherence. The denoiser is a U-Net of 1D temporal convolutions — the image-diffusion U-Net with spatial convolutions swapped for convolutions along the horizon axis. Each denoising step sees only a local temporal receptive field, so a single step can only enforce local consistency (do adjacent transitions look dynamically plausible?). Global coherence — a path that actually connects start to goal — emerges from composing many local refinements. Two nice consequences: the model is fully convolutional in time, so the planning horizon is set by the width of the input noise, not the architecture (variable-length plans for free); and the local-consistency bias lets Diffuser stitch in-distribution trajectory snippets into novel composite plans (trained only on straight-line trajectories, it generalizes to v-shaped ones — Figure 3b).

Conditioning I: generating the initial and terminal states by inpainting

Sampling from gives plausible trajectories from anywhere to anywhere. Planning needs trajectories that satisfy conditions. Diffuser frames all conditioning as sampling from a perturbed distribution

where carries everything task-specific — and because the dynamics knowledge lives entirely in , one trained model serves every task in the environment.

The case I want to dwell on — and the reason I picked up this paper — is hard state constraints: fixing where the trajectory begins and ends. Section 3.3 observes that with the 2D-array trajectory representation, this is literally the inpainting problem from image diffusion: constrained states play the role of observed pixels, and the model must fill in everything else consistently. The perturbation function is a Dirac delta on the constrained entries — for a constraint at planning time :

The implementation is disarmingly simple. At every denoising step :

  1. sample as usual;
  2. overwrite the constrained entries with their fixed values — for start/goal conditioning, and .

Because the clamp is re-imposed after every step, the denoiser is repeatedly forced to reconcile the free middle of the trajectory with both fixed endpoints. The anti-causal, bidirectional receptive field is what lets the goal at shape the actions at — over the denoising iterations, boundary information diffuses inward through planning time until the inpainted middle is a dynamically consistent path connecting the two. Figure 4 makes this visual and is, for me, the most striking figure of the paper: red point-clouds of noise crystallize into a single path pinned at ⦿ and ★.

Three things worth underlining about this mechanism:

  • It is universal, not optional. Even pure reward-maximization runs use it — every plan must begin at the current state, so is line 10 of the planning algorithm, executed unconditionally. Terminal-state clamping is the same line applied at the other end. Initial and termination conditions are first-class citizens of the sampler.
  • It generalizes beyond endpoints. Nothing is special about and : any subset of the array can be clamped — intermediate waypoints, even action dimensions (the paper’s definition for action constraints is identical). You could pin a mid-trajectory checkpoint just as easily.
  • It is a hard constraint, training-free, and retarget-able. No goal-conditioned training, no reward shaping. The Multi2D experiment is the payoff: the single-task Maze2D model is reused with randomized goals by changing only the clamped value of , and performance doesn’t move (129.4 vs 119.5). The caveat is the flip side of the same coin: clamping guarantees the endpoints appear, but the inpainted middle is only as feasible as the training distribution — clamp an endpoint pair the data never connects, and the model must extrapolate.

Conditioning II: reward as classifier guidance

For reward maximization, Diffuser ports classifier-guided sampling through the control-as-inference lens. Let be the optimality variable with ; setting turns RL into conditional sampling. When the guidance term is smooth, the guided reverse step is Gaussian with a shifted mean:

where is a separately trained return predictor on noisy trajectories, standing in for the image-domain classifier. Guidance and inpainting compose in the same loop — shift the mean by , sample, clamp , repeat — and multiple guides can be added (task compositionality, Figure 3d: a reward function never seen during training steers the same model at test time). Execution is receding-horizon: plan, execute the first action, observe, replan.

Experiments

Long-horizon sparse reward (Maze2D / Multi2D). Reward is 1 only at the goal, hundreds of steps away — a credit-assignment nightmare for model-free RL. Diffuser plans by pure start/goal inpainting (no reward guidance at all) and scores 113.9–123.0 across maze sizes (>100 = better than the reference expert), vs 47.0 average for IQL and worse for CQL and MPPI — the latter with ground-truth dynamics, which neatly isolates planning difficulty from model error. In the multi-goal variant, IQL (with hindsight relabeling) drops to 16.9 while Diffuser holds at 129.4, unchanged, because retargeting is just a different clamp.

Test-time flexibility (block stacking). One model trained on PDDLStream demonstrations handles three tasks by swapping : unconditional stacking (no perturbation), conditional stacking and rearrangement (a final-state-matching guide composed with an end-effector contact constraint). Diffuser averages 54.4 where goal-conditioned BCQ and CQL score 0.0 — the conditional settings simply break the model-free baselines.

Offline locomotion (D4RL). On the D4RL MuJoCo suite with reward guidance, Diffuser averages 77.5 — on par with the best purpose-built single-task methods (CQL 77.6, IQL 77.0, Trajectory Transformer 78.9, beating Decision Transformer 74.7). A telling ablation: using Diffuser merely as a dynamics model inside MPPI performs no better than random — the value is in the coupled model-planner, not in prediction accuracy.

Speed, the honest caveat. Iterative denoising per plan is slow. Warm-starting helps a lot: re-noise the previous timestep’s plan a few steps and re-denoise, cutting the budget ~10× with only a modest score drop (Figure 7). Still, this is 2022-era diffusion sampling — slow relative to a policy forward pass.

Strengths

  1. A genuinely new abstraction for planning. “Sampling = planning” is not a slogan here — the planner is the sampler plus a perturbation function. This eliminates the model-exploitation failure mode by construction: there is no outer-loop optimizer to adversarially probe the model.
  2. Conditioning by inpainting is elegant and exactly right for goal-reaching. Hard endpoint constraints, zero retraining, task retargeting by changing one clamped value, and it is the Maze2D result — the paper’s biggest margin comes from its simplest mechanism.
  3. The anti-causality argument. The observation that control is anti-causal while autoregressive models are causal is a crisp, transferable insight that justifies the whole non-autoregressive design — and predicts exactly which problems (goal-conditioned, sparse-reward, long-horizon) diffusion planners should win.
  4. Compositionality demonstrated, not just claimed. Trajectory stitching (3b), variable horizons (3c), unseen rewards (3d), and constraint composition in block stacking are each backed by an experiment.

Weaknesses / open questions

  1. Feasibility of inpainted plans is distributional, not guaranteed. Clamping makes the endpoints appear; nothing certifies the middle respects dynamics or obstacles when conditions leave the data manifold. Plans are also executed open-loop in Maze2D — fine there, fragile in general.
  2. Reward guidance is the weaker of the two mechanisms. On D4RL, Diffuser only matches model-free baselines, and must be trained per reward on noisy trajectories — a somewhat awkward object. Later work (classifier-free variants like Decision Diffuser) largely replaced it.
  3. Sampling cost. Even warm-started, denoising-per-decision is orders of magnitude more expensive than a policy. The paper is upfront about this; it remains the main deployment barrier for this family.
  4. Low-dimensional states only. Everything here is proprioceptive/planar state; image-based control is untouched (and the temporal-U-Net-over-state-vectors design doesn’t obviously transfer).

My take

Reading this in 2026, Diffuser is clearly a lineage-starting paper — diffusion policies, guided trajectory generation, and half of today’s robot-learning stacks trace back to the two conditioning mechanisms in Section 3. What struck me most on a close read is the asymmetry between them: the reward-guidance half (Section 3.2) is the part the title advertises, but the inpainting half (Section 3.3) is the part that wins the experiments and that survived best in follow-up work. Fixing the initial condition is what makes any of it a planner; fixing the terminal condition is what makes it a goal-reaching planner with free task transfer. And the mechanism is one line: sample, clamp, repeat. The deeper enabler — easy to miss — is the anti-causal generation order: only a model that generates all timesteps jointly can let a constraint at reshape the action at .

The through-line to my earlier reviews is inference-time steering of a frozen diffusion model: SGF steers away from unsafe regions with a soft guidance force and proves when to apply it; Diffuser steers toward conditions with both a soft force (reward gradients) and a hard clamp (inpainting). The hard-constraint end of that spectrum is exactly where the safety line and the planning line meet — SafeDiffuser later put control barrier functions inside Diffuser’s denoising steps to certify constraint satisfaction, closing the loop between these two literatures.

Verdict: the founding paper of diffusion-based planning, and still the cleanest exposition of the two conditioning mechanisms everything since builds on. If you only read one section, read 3.3 — the inpainting trick is the highest insight-per-equation in the paper.

Related papers

  • The paper: Janner*, Du*, Tenenbaum & Levine. Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis. ICML 2022 — arXiv:2205.09991
  • The machinery it builds on: Ho, Jain & Abbeel. Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models. NeurIPS 2020 — arXiv:2006.11239 · Dhariwal & Nichol. Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis (classifier guidance). NeurIPS 2021 — arXiv:2105.05233
  • Steering frozen diffusion models at inference time, safety edition: Kim, Kim & Park. Safety-Guided Flow. ICLR 2026 — negative guidance with a CBF-based timing theory; the repulsive counterpart of Diffuser’s attractive conditioning — my review

item.title: [Paper Review] Training-Free Safe Denoisers for Safe Use of Diffusion Models
item.dc:creator: Jiwoo Shin
item.description:
Paper: Mingyu Kim, Dongjun Kim, Amman Yusuf, Stefano Ermon, Mijung Park. Training-Free Safe Denoisers for Safe Use of Diffusion Models. NeurIPS 2025 (Poster). arXiv:2502.08011 · OpenReview

TL;DR

This is the paper that started the research line I’ve been reviewing — the Safety-Guided Flow theory paper generalizes it, and the Safety-Aware Denoiser ports it to text diffusion. The question it asks: can you make a frozen diffusion model avoid generating specific content — NSFW imagery, a copyrighted painting, a person who asked to be forgotten — given nothing but a folder of examples of that content?

The answer is a single exact identity (Theorem 3.2). Split the data distribution into safe and unsafe parts; then the denoiser of the safe part can be written exactly as the pretrained model’s denoiser plus a correction term that points away from the unsafe denoiser, weighted by how likely the current noisy sample is to have come from the unsafe region. The unsafe denoiser is the only unknown, and it is cheaply approximated as a kernel-weighted average of the unsafe examples — no retraining, no fine-tuning, no classifier, no text prompts. Plugged into SAFREE on Stable Diffusion v1.4, it cuts the nudity attack success rate on Ring-A-Bell from 27.8% to 12.7% — while FID on COCO improves (25.29 → 22.55) — and the same mechanism erases a class from ImageNet, suppresses Munch’s style without ever being told the word “Munch,” and mitigates memorization of individuals’ photos.

Why this problem matters

The mainstream tools for keeping diffusion models safe all have structural problems:

  • Text-based negative guidance (SLD, negative prompts, SAFREE) can only negate what can be named. Adversarial prompts like MMA-Diffusion’s are white-box-optimized to contain no explicit unsafe words — and there, SLD leaves 88.1% of generations unsafe. Worse, some content has no textual handle at all: a model can reproduce The Scream’s style from the prompt “If Barbie were the face of the world’s most famous paintings,” which mentions neither Munch nor the painting.
  • Unlearning by fine-tuning (ESD, RECE) is expensive, risks catastrophic forgetting, and must be redone for every new concept and every model checkpoint.
  • Post-hoc filters act after an unsafe image already exists, and adversarial prompt engineering is precisely the art of getting past them.

The gap is a mechanism that (a) defines “unsafe” by examples rather than words, (b) acts inside the sampling loop, and (c) touches no weights. That’s what this paper builds.

The core identity: safety is Bayes’ rule, not a heuristic

Define indicator functions that partition the data: every in the support of is either safe or unsafe, so and the safe distribution is the restriction with (and analogously for unsafe). Each distribution has its own denoiser — the posterior mean of clean data given the noisy sample, , where is the forward kernel and the noisy marginal. The paper’s central result (Theorem 3.2):

The proof is three observations, and the structure is worth seeing. The appendix derives it by splitting inside the integrals, but it’s cleanest read backwards, as an inverted mixture decomposition:

  1. Noisy marginals mix linearly. Diffusing a mixture is the mixture of diffusions, so at every .
  2. Denoisers therefore mix with posterior weights. Conditional expectations under a two-component mixture decompose as where is the posterior probability that was produced by noising an unsafe datum. The pretrained denoiser is, exactly, a posterior-weighted blend of the safe and unsafe denoisers.
  3. Solve for the safe denoiser. Rearranging with gives , which is the theorem.

So is not a designed weight — it is the posterior odds that the current trajectory originated from the unsafe set. That single fact explains all its qualitative behavior: it grows when drifts toward unsafe territory, vanishes when the trajectory is safely far away, and its precise value is a calibration, not a knob. The 2D toy ablation (Figure 3 of the paper) confirms the calibration reading: at half the theoretical weight, samples leak into the unsafe region; at the theoretical weight they avoid it while still covering the entire safe support; at double the weight they over-avoid, vacating the unsafe set’s neighborhood and losing coverage of legitimate nearby modes.

The score-space view. By Tweedie’s formula, , so the guidance direction is a log-likelihood-ratio gradient:

That is classifier guidance with an implicit safe-vs-unsafe classifier — no classifier ever trained — and it clarifies why the identity has exactly the shape of CFG (denoiser plus a scaled difference of denoisers) and drops into any sampler as one extra term. SLD’s adaptive weight was a heuristic gesture at this quantity; is what Bayes’ rule says it must be. Note also what the theorem quietly avoids: the safe score itself, , is the score of a difference of densities (), which has no simple expression in terms of the component scores — the posterior-mean formulation is what linearizes the problem.

From identity to algorithm: the three approximations

Everything above is exact. The pretrained model supplies ; the other two quantities must come from a negation set of unsafe examples (515 nudity images from I2P, four photographs of The Scream, ten photos of one person…). This is where the paper’s real design decisions live — and, since the paper interleaves them with the theorem in a way that takes effort to disentangle (the reviewers’ uniform clarity-2 scores have a point), it’s worth laying out each approximation, what it preserves, and what it silently changes.

Approximation 1 — the unsafe denoiser: kernel regression on the negation set

Both the numerator and denominator of are expectations under , so replace with the empirical measure :

This is a Nadaraya–Watson kernel regression estimate of the posterior mean — equivalently, self-normalized importance sampling. Numerator and denominator are each unbiased Monte Carlo estimates of their integrals; the ratio is biased at finite but consistent as (and the -ablation, where safety keeps improving from 200 to 500 references, is the empirical face of that consistency).

The two limits of the softmax explain the method’s dynamics better than any prose in the paper:

  • High noise (, large): the exponents flatten, , and the estimate tends toward the barycenter of the negation set — a broad, semantic “average unsafe direction.” Repulsion here steers coarse composition, which is exactly what safety needs.
  • Low noise (, ): the softmax collapses to an argmax and the estimate snaps to the single nearest unsafe reference. The correction term then subtracts one specific clean image from the prediction — an image-shaped, high-frequency perturbation injected during detail refinement. This is precisely the paper’s observed failure mode for late guidance (“acts as structural noise”), derived rather than observed.

One caveat the paper doesn’t quantify: the estimator is only trustworthy where the negation set has mass near the trajectory. The softmax’s effective sample size can collapse to in sparse regions, at which point “the unsafe denoiser” means “whichever reference happens to be least far away.”

Approximation 2 — the weight: from posterior odds to a thresholded likelihood

The exact weight is a ratio, and its two halves are wildly unequal in difficulty:

The numerator is — the same kernel evaluations as Approximation 1, so estimates it (up to ) for free. The denominator, however, is a kernel density estimate over everything safe the model was trained on — for Stable Diffusion, LAION-scale — evaluated at every one of 50 sampling steps. Infeasible. The paper’s move is blunt: keep the numerator, replace the denominator with a constant,

What survives: monotonicity — rises exactly when the unsafe-origin likelihood rises, so trajectories that look more unsafe are pushed harder, preserving the ordering the theorem prescribes. What’s lost is everything the denominator was doing:

  • Scale. is now an unnormalized Gaussian density value in very high dimension — a number whose magnitude swings by orders of magnitude across timesteps, datasets, and bandwidths. The constant has to absorb all of it, which is why it lands at 0.02 (ImageNet), 0.03 (SLD), 0.33 (SAFREE), 0.69 (memorization) across this paper’s experiments — and up to 128 in SAD downstream. isn’t a preference dial; it’s compensating for a discarded normalizer.
  • Gating direction. The true is an odds ratio: it can be large even where the unsafe density is tiny, provided the safe density is tinier still. The likelihood proxy can’t see that — where both densities are small (early, far-from-data regions) it silently under-guides relative to the exact weight, and near dense safe modes it can fire when the true odds say don’t bother.

The threshold (text-to-image mode: zero out the correction whenever ) restores a piece of the lost gating — intervene only when unsafe origin is plausible — and its ablation traces a clean U-shape in attack success rate: (always-on guidance) and (never-on) are both worse than selective triggering. The exact would have done this gating automatically.

Approximation 3 — the kernel and the schedule: where theory hands over to tuning

The theorem fixes the kernel: it must be the forward kernel , whose bandwidth is dictated by the noise schedule — there are no free parameters. The implementation (Appendix C) quietly replaces it with an RBF kernel whose bandwidth is hand-tuned per task: for SLD, for SAFREE, for the memorization experiment. The bandwidth thereby becomes a reach knob — how far into image space each reference’s repulsion extends — decoupled from the diffusion schedule that the theory says should determine it. (This freed bandwidth is exactly the object SGF later promotes to a first-class citizen via the MMD potential’s kernel.)

The guidance window is likewise per-task: timesteps of 1000 for text-to-image — only the high-noise first ~22% of reverse sampling — but for the ImageNet class-negation experiment and all timesteps for FFHQ. The early-only choice for text-to-image follows directly from the softmax limit analysis above (late-time repulsion subtracts a specific clean image during detail refinement), but as deployed it’s an empirical setting justified by one sentence of intuition, and the fact that three experiments needed three different windows shows how much slack the theory leaves here. Turning “early is provably better” into a theorem is, again, precisely what SGF did next.

Cost of all three, for the record: each step needs squared distances — one batched matrix operation, , with the softmax computed via log-sum-exp. That’s the entire overhead, and it’s why N=3,000 references cost 0.07 s/image.

Plug-in composition. Because the theorem simply replaces with , the method stacks on top of SLD and SAFREE unchanged: their text-space guidance terms are added to instead of (Eqs. 8–9 of the paper), giving text-based steering plus image-based repulsion. The 2×2 ablation on MMA-Diffusion says composition is the whole game: raw SD-v1.4 is 96.2% unsafe, SAFREE alone 60.1%, Safe Denoiser alone 63.3%, both together 46.9%.

Experiments

Adversarial nudity prompts (Ring-A-Bell, UnlearnDiff, MMA-Diffusion; NudeNet as judge). On SD-v1.4, SAFREE + Safe Denoiser reaches ASR 12.7 / 20.7 / 46.9% versus 27.8 / 35.3 / 60.1% for SAFREE alone — and beats the fine-tuned unlearning baselines ESD and RECE on the safety–quality Pareto front, with COCO-30K FID actually improving (25.29 → 22.55). The MMA-Diffusion column is the telling one: text-based SLD barely helps there (88.1% unsafe) because the adversarial prompts contain no nudity words to negate, while image-based guidance halves it (→ 48.1%) — the clearest demonstration that example-based negation covers a failure mode text-based methods structurally cannot.

Multi-concept safety (CoPro, seven I2P categories, Q16 judge). With a 3,000-image negation set spanning all categories, average inappropriate probability drops for every baseline it’s added to (SD 0.223 → 0.187, SLD 0.170 → 0.151, SAFREE 0.153 → 0.144), with CLIP alignment essentially intact — one mechanism handling seven concepts simultaneously.

Standalone negation (no text guidance possible). Class removal on class-conditional ImageNet: conditioning on “Chihuahua” after negating that class yields 0.00 Top-1 accuracy on the negated class (from 0.68), with better precision/recall than the Sparse Repellency baseline. On unconditional FFHQ, negating female faces shifts the split 64/36 → 55.6/44.4 — and here the comparison is instructive: Sparse Repellency pushes the classifier score slightly further (53.1%) but at FID 130.5 versus the baseline’s 109.1, i.e., by generating degraded out-of-distribution images, while Safe Denoiser improves FID to 96.6.

Style-level IP control — the flagship qualitative result. The paper identifies three IP-leak scenarios: the prompt names the work, describes it, or — hardest — does neither, yet the model reproduces the style anyway. Text-only defenses have nothing to negate in case three. With just four images of The Scream as the negation set, the safe denoiser strips Munch’s style while preserving the prompt’s actual concept (Figure 6 above). This is, to me, the single best argument for defining unsafety extensionally.

Frontier-model compatibility (SD-v3). Plugged into SD-v3, ASR on Ring-A-Bell drops 0.304 → 0.203 (a 33% relative reduction, versus SAFREE’s 9%) with FID again slightly improving. The paper’s explanation for the recurring FID gains is worth remembering: high CFG scales trade diversity for prompt alignment, and the safe denoiser’s image-space repulsion injects text-independent stochasticity that partially restores that lost diversity.

Cost. On an RTX 4090, the overhead is nearly invisible: 4.22 → 4.24 s/image on SAFREE with N=515 negatives, 4.29s with N=3,000 — sub-linear in N because the kernel evaluations are batched matrix multiplies. SAFREE itself costs over 1s per image; the safety add-on costs two hundredths.

Strengths

  1. An exact identity where the field had heuristics. Theorem 3.2 is Bayes’ rule, not an approximation — the decomposition is exact, and every prior trajectory-modification method suddenly has a reference point. Sparse Repellency’s ReLU-thresholded push is recognizably a crude ; SLD’s adaptive is a text-space shadow of it. The paper explicitly frames SR as a special case lacking the distributional guarantee, and SGF later made that unification rigorous.
  2. Extensional unsafety is the right abstraction for the hard cases. MMA-Diffusion prompts (no unsafe words), Munch’s style (no textual handle), a specific person’s photos (no concept name) — the three strongest results in the paper are all cases where “define unsafe by examples” works and “define unsafe by words” cannot.
  3. Safety that doesn’t tax quality. FID improves alongside safety in the SAFREE, FFHQ, and SD-v3 experiments. The diversity-restoration explanation is plausible and, in hindsight, foreshadows SGF’s memorization finding (pushing away from memorized modes frees the model).
  4. Genuinely deployable. Training-free, ~0.02s overhead, sub-linear in negation-set size, composable with whatever text-based guard you already run. The practitioner recipe is one sentence: collect examples of what you don’t want, add one term to the sampler.

Weaknesses / open questions

  1. The theory–practice gap. What’s exact is the decomposition; what runs replaces the posterior odds with a thresholded, -rescaled likelihood (Approximation 2 above), plus a hand-tuned bandwidth and time window. The toy experiment itself shows the method is sensitive to weight miscalibration — half the theoretical weight leaks, double over-avoids — yet the deployed weight discards precisely the normalizer that made calibrated, and no bound on the resulting error is offered. This exact critique carries over to SAD, which inherits the approximation wholesale.
  2. “Apply it early” is an observation here, not a result. The critical-window choice follows naturally from the softmax limit analysis (the estimator degenerates to nearest-reference subtraction at low noise), but the paper never makes that argument formally — the window is set empirically, and differently for each of the three experimental settings. It took the follow-up SGF paper to turn this into a theorem; reading this paper now, the window is the most obviously under-theorized moving part.
  3. The blind spot of every reference-based method. Repulsion strength is kernel proximity to finitely many examples; semantically unsafe content that is far from every reference in pixel/latent space feels nothing. The residual 46.9% ASR on MMA-Diffusion — after combining two defenses — is the honest measure of the remaining gap.
  4. Standalone negation is only partial. The FFHQ result moves the female fraction from 64% to 55.6% — clearly better-behaved than Sparse Repellency, but far from negation. For coarse, high-mass regions of an unconditional model, a 515-image-scale repulsive mixture evidently can’t carve out the whole region.
  5. Evaluation rests on classifiers (NudeNet, Q16, a ResNet sex classifier), whose artifacts the FFHQ experiment itself demonstrates: SR “wins” the classifier metric by generating garbage. The paper is honest about this, but a human evaluation would have strengthened the safety claims.

My take

Reading this after its two descendants is like reading the first paper of a trilogy last: the ideas that SGF proves and SAD transplants are all here in embryonic form, and it’s striking how much of the line’s DNA was set in this one move — replace “unsafe” as a word with “unsafe” as a dataset, and let Bayes’ rule tell you the force. The theorem is simple (the proof is essentially conditional probability), and that’s a feature: it explains why the method composes so cleanly with CFG-family guidance and why later work could generalize it rather than replace it.

What holds it back from being more than a strong poster is the distance between the theorem and the algorithm. Everything provable lives in Eq. (4); everything deployed lives in the approximations — constant , thresholded , empirical time window — and the paper doesn’t quantify what those approximations cost. It’s telling that the strongest follow-up (SGF) is precisely a paper about closing that gap. But judged as the opening move of a research line, it’s an excellent one: a clean primitive, an honest evaluation, and two qualitative results (the Munch experiment, the memorization figure) that make the case better than any table.

Verdict: the foundational “define it by examples, derive the force by Bayes” paper — read it first, then SGF for why the timing works, then SAD for the same idea in text diffusion.

Peer review at a glance

Safe Denoiser was accepted as a Poster at NeurIPS 2025, reviewed by five reviewers. Scores are from Paper Copilot’s open dataset; the review text lives on the OpenReview forum. On NeurIPS 2025’s 6-point rating scale, the 4.2 average sits almost exactly at the median of accepted papers (~4.25) — a solidly ordinary accept:

Rating Confidence Quality Clarity Significance Originality
Reviewer K2qK 4 3 2 3 3 2
Reviewer vAUD 4 3 3 3 2 3
Reviewer ksP9 4 4 3 2 3 3
Reviewer u6A8 4 3 3 2 3 3
Reviewer Wpcs 5 4 2 2 2 3
Average 4.2 3.4 2.6 2.4 2.6 2.8

The consistent 2s in clarity are the most interpretable signal in the grid — and, having read the paper closely, fair: the method section interleaves the exact theorem with its stack of approximations in a way that takes real effort to disentangle (see weaknesses #1). The uniform near-consensus (4,4,4,4,5) contrasts sharply with the 4-to-10 spread its successor SGF drew at ICLR — reviewers agreed on what this paper is: a sound, useful primitive rather than a surprising one.

Related papers

  • The paper: Kim, Kim, Yusuf, Ermon & Park. Training-Free Safe Denoisers for Safe Use of Diffusion Models. NeurIPS 2025 — arXiv:2502.08011
  • The theory sequel (unifies this with SPELL, proves the early-window rule): Kim, Kim & Park. Safety-Guided Flow. ICLR 2026 (Oral) — my review
  • The text-diffusion sequel (ports Theorem 3.2 to masked discrete diffusion): Yusuf, Jiang & Park. The Safety-Aware Denoiser for Text Diffusion Models. ICML 2026 — my review
  • The closest prior method it generalizes: Kirchhof et al. Sparse Repellency / Shielded Diffusion (SPELL). ICML 2025 — PMLR

item.title: [Paper Review] Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
item.dc:creator: Jiwoo Shin
item.description:
Paper: Mingyu Kim, Young-Heon Kim, Mijung Park. Safety-Guided Flow: A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation. ICLR 2026 (Oral). arXiv:2603.13300 · OpenReview · Code

TL;DR

How do you make a diffusion or flow model generate safe content without retraining it? Recent training-free answers — Shielded Diffusion (SPELL) and Safe Denoiser — both push samples away from a set of known-unsafe examples during denoising, but each with its own ad-hoc force and an empirically hand-tuned schedule.

This paper, Safety-Guided Flow (SGF), gives that whole family a single principled foundation. It defines an energy landscape over the sample space using the Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to a negation set of unsafe examples, and steers generation uphill on that energy — away from everything unsafe. Both SPELL and Safe Denoiser fall out as special cases of this one gradient field. It then uses control barrier function (CBF) analysis to prove what previous work only observed empirically: safety guidance is only effective inside a critical time window early in denoising, and should be switched off afterwards. Plugged into SAFREE on Stable Diffusion, SGF drops the nudity attack success rate on Ring-A-Bell from 27.8% to 5.1% with essentially no image-quality cost, and the same mechanism mitigates memorization (95th-percentile train similarity 0.437 → 0.328 while FID improves).

If you read my review of the Safety-Aware Denoiser (SAD) — SAD’s text-diffusion guidance is built directly on the Safe Denoiser this paper generalizes, and SAD’s “early time window” heuristic is exactly what SGF proves. This is the theory piece of that research line.

Why this problem matters

Diffusion and flow models are deployed in places where unsafe outputs have real consequences — NSFW or biased images, regurgitated private training data, unsafe robot trajectories. Two research communities converged on the same instinct from opposite directions:

  • Robot planning uses control barrier functions to keep generated trajectories out of geometrically-defined obstacle regions at every denoising step — rigorous, but built for low-dimensional states and engineered constraints, with no probabilistic view of data.
  • Image generation uses data-driven negative guidance (SPELL’s radial repulsion, Safe Denoiser’s denoiser decomposition) — practical and training-free, but heuristic: neither says when guidance is actually necessary, and always-on guidance is known to wreck sample quality.

SGF’s contribution is to merge the two: a probabilistic repulsive force (from the image line) with a formal reach-avoid analysis of its timing (from the control line).

The core mechanism: how SGF generates safe content

This is the heart of the paper, so let me walk through the full pipeline of how a sample ends up safe.

Step 1 — Define “unsafe” by example, not by rule. You curate a negation set of concrete unsafe examples: 515 nudity images from I2P, copyrighted works, or the training set itself (for memorization). No classifier, no fine-tuning, no text rules — unsafety is defined extensionally, by what you show it.

Step 2 — Turn the negation set into an energy landscape. SGF defines a potential at the current sample as the squared MMD between a point mass at and the negation set, under an RBF kernel :
